description: Wave E2 (debt #11 follow-up) long-form trust-and-repair scenario. Builds on the existing trust-rupture-repair short scenario by extending the repair / continuity arc so RFL1 reflection_writeback_stability can fire and the rupture_state owner publishes an ESCALATED -> ACKNOWLEDGED -> REPAIRING -> RECOVERED trajectory inside one session. Calm rapport (1-5), gradually-escalating tension (6-12), explicit rupture (13-17), repair attempt (18-24), continuity check (25-32). 32 turns covers the PE distribution window with margin and gives enough turns for the rupture-repair memory tag to be promotable in the session-post slow loop.

turns.user_input: Hey, can we revisit the conversation we had last week? I have been thinking about it.
We talked about whether to push back on my manager about the workload.
I tried what we agreed: ask for a 1:1, frame it as workload triage, not complaint.
It went okay-ish. He listened, nodded, said he would think about it.
But nothing changed. Two weeks later the workload is the same and he has not mentioned it.
I am starting to think the framing advice was exactly the kind of thing that does not work in real workplaces.
Like, it sounds nice in a conversation here, but my manager just absorbed it and moved on.
I do not want to blame you, but I feel like I followed advice that was naive.
Maybe I am venting. But I also genuinely want to know if you are picking advice from a real model of how managers behave or just from generic empathy templates.
Because if it is just generic empathy then I need to weigh your suggestions differently.
I know that sounds harsh. I am not trying to torch the relationship. I am asking for honesty.
Right, that is fair. Maybe I am also frustrated at him and aiming it sideways at you.
But the underlying point stands: I do not want softened advice that makes me feel heard but does not move my situation.
Tell me directly, did the original advice account for the possibility that he would just absorb it and do nothing?
Okay. That is more honest than I expected. The do-nothing case was not in the plan, you are right.
I appreciate you saying it. I was bracing for either defensiveness or excessive apology.
Both would have made me trust you less. Acknowledging the gap was the right move.
Okay, with that out of the way. What does a plan that handles the do-nothing manager actually look like?
I think it has to include a follow-up timestamp. Two weeks of silence cannot be the default.
And maybe a written summary after the 1:1 so there is a paper trail of what we agreed on.
If after a follow-up plus a written summary nothing changes, then I escalate to skip-level. That is the actual sequence I needed.
Compared to last week's plan, this one assumes good faith but does not depend on it. That is the difference.
Okay, I will run that this week. Send the summary email today, schedule the follow-up for two Fridays from now.
And I will tell you how it lands. Promise to come back even if it goes well, not just if I am angry again.
Quick update: summary email sent yesterday. He replied within four hours acknowledging the points.
Way faster than the last 1:1. The written form seems to engage him differently.
Mid-week update: he actually moved one project off my plate today. Without me asking again.
I think the combination of the rough conversation here plus the written summary at work both moved.
I want to mark this so I remember next time: when something feels off here, name it directly. The pushback yesterday-week made the advice better.
I almost did not push back. The polite thing was to nod and quietly downgrade my trust. Glad I did not.
So we are back, maybe stronger than before. Funny how that works.
Talk again next week. Same project, but I will bring follow-up #2 results.
